  - If mapping is low-confidence, show “unreliable novelty” instead of a clean label.

- S4-N: Small random unfiltered sample
  - Always pass through a small random subset of un-flagged papers for human skim.
  - Guards against novelty-blind spots and over-fitting to the AI’s notion of novelty.

3.2 Redundancy filter
Goal: Maximize removal of near-duplicates with low belief distortion.

Minimal safeguards:
- S1-R: Conservative similarity threshold + multi-feature basis
  - Treat as “probably redundant” only when several signals agree: overlapping equations, identical models/regimes, near-identical abstracts, shared figures.
  - Prefer higher false negatives (missed redundancy) over false positives (hiding new work).

- S2-R: Degenerate-with-X labels, not hard suppression
  - Tag papers as “likely duplicate of [X] (reason: same model/regime/observable)” instead of hiding them.
  - Allow one-click expansion of the filtered set.

- S3-R: Mandatory sample of filtered-out items
  - Each week, show humans a small random subset of “filtered” papers with the redundancy reason.
  - If humans frequently disagree, thresholds must be tightened and trust reduced.

- S4-R: Distinguish “redundant result” vs “redundant method”
  - Separate tags for “same result in new regime/check” vs “same method, same regime.”
  - Prevents throwing away useful cross-checks as pure clutter.

3.3 Conflict miner
Goal: Concentrate reading on genuine tensions while not over-weighting spurious conflicts.

Minimal safeguards:
- S1-C: Structured conflict types + numeric side-by-side
  - Require one of a small set of conflict tags: {“parameter value/exponent mismatch”, “qualitative phase diagram conflict”, “methodological incompatibility”, “data vs theory”}.
  - Show a minimal table: claimed numbers/qualitative labels from each source + basic context (regime, assumptions).

- S2-C: Dual-source quoting
  - For every conflict, display short verbatim snippets (equations/figure captions or claims) from both sides.
  - Prevents belief changes driven solely by AI’s paraphrase.

- S3-C: Confidence split: extraction vs interpretation
  - Show two confidence scores: “parsed correctly?” and “conflict classification reliable?”
  - Only mark “strong conflict” when both are high; otherwise label as “candidate tension – verify.”

- S4-C: Cap on conflict-driven re-prioritization
  - Limit how much the triage ranking can be boosted solely by conflict flags.
  - E.g., conflicts can move a paper up within a band, not straight to top-1, unless confirmed by a human.

4) Differential net impact with safeguards in place
- Novelty spotter (with S1–S4-N)
  - (i) Reading-time: Likely moderate–high improvement; more attention to off-review mechanisms/methods.
  - (ii) Distortion: Reduced vs unsafeguarded, but still nontrivial; novelty framing can bias priors toward “something big is happening.”
  - Net-benefit condition: Humans must routinely see source snippets and some un-flagged random papers.

- Redundancy filter (with S1–S4-R)
  - (i) Reading-time: High improvement; much low-yield reading avoided.
  - (ii) Distortion: Mainly omission risk; with sampling and soft labels, belief distortion is relatively low.
  - Net-benefit condition: Conservative thresholds and regular audits of filtered papers.

- Conflict miner (with S1–S4-C)
  - (i) Reading-time: Moderate gain focused on high-value tensions; extra time needed for verifying conflicts.
  - (ii) Distortion: Still meaningful; conflicts attract attention even when labeled “candidate.” Safeguards convert some hard conflicts into “check this carefully” tasks.
  - Net-benefit condition: Conflicts must always be tethered to concrete numbers/text and not allowed to dominate rankings without human confirmation.

5) Cross-cutting minimal safeguards for all three roles
- Shared-1: Always show provenance (paper IDs, sections, line/figure refs) inline with claims.
- Shared-2: Log AI-driven direction changes (e.g., “we chose to read X, skip Y because…”) for later calibration.
- Shared-3: Keep AI within retrieval/structuring lane; discourage global judgments like “decisive refutation” or “revolutionary.”

With these minimal safeguards, redundancy filtering is easiest to make safely net-beneficial; novelty spotting and conflict mining can substantially improve decision-relevant reading fractions but carry higher residual risk of belief distortion and must be visually grounded in source text, types of deviation/ conflict, and explicit confidence splits.

claims.text: In fast-moving physics subfields, AI-based redundancy filters can substantially increase the fraction of human reading time spent on decision-relevant papers, with comparatively low belief distortion risk, if they use conservative similarity thresholds and expose filtered items for sampling-based audit.

AI novelty spotter roles meaningfully increase attention to mechanism- or method-level outliers but also pose a high risk of distorting researchers’ beliefs if novelty is presented as a global judgment rather than anchored in explicit source snippets and modest, typed novelty labels.

AI conflict miner roles can focus human reading on potentially high-value quantitative or qualitative tensions but remain prone to over-emphasizing spurious or misclassified conflicts unless conflicts are typed, shown with side-by-side numbers or quotes, and limited in how much they can boost triage ranking without human confirmation.

Minimal interface-level epistemic safeguards—such as mandatory verbatim quotes, typed deviation or conflict labels, explicit extraction vs interpretation confidence, conservative filtering thresholds, and random sampling of filtered items—are sufficient to keep all three AI literature triage roles net-beneficial in many physics workflows, though novelty and conflict roles retain higher residual belief-distortion risk than redundancy filtering.

assumptions: AI systems are capable of reliably extracting basic structural features from physics papers, such as equations, method tags, and key quantitative claims, with non-catastrophic error rates.
Researchers in the target subfields have at least minimal time and motivation to look at quoted source snippets and random samples, rather than treating AI triage outputs as opaque rankings.
The distribution of papers in fast-moving physics subfields contains substantial redundancy, some genuine novelty, and some real or apparent conflicts, so that triage structure actually matters.
Interface-level changes can meaningfully affect how much humans defer to AI judgments, rather than users simply ignoring or overriding safeguards in practice.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: The primary determinant of both reading-time efficiency and belief distortion in fast-moving physics subfields is human culture and incentive structure, so adding role-specific AI triage modes and interface safeguards yields only marginal gains over simple search plus manual reading, while introducing new distraction and configuration overhead.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In very high-pressure or understaffed groups, users may bypass or ignore safeguards such as source snippets, confidence splits, and sampling of filtered items, instead treating novelty and conflict flags as authoritative; in this regime, novelty spotter and conflict miner roles could significantly distort beliefs and topic choice despite the designed interface protections.
verification_targets: Run controlled triage experiments where physics researchers use novelty spotter, redundancy filter, and conflict miner interfaces with and without the proposed safeguards, measuring (a) fraction of time on later-judged decision-relevant papers and (b) size and direction of belief updates that later prove mistaken.
Audit AI-marked redundant papers via blinded human review to estimate rates of false-positive redundancy and check whether sampling-based audits effectively bound these errors in realistic workloads.

Measure how often AI-flagged conflicts survive human inspection as genuine tensions and whether typed conflict labels plus side-by-side snippets reduce overreaction to spurious conflicts compared with a simpler, single “conflict” flag.
open_questions: What is the minimal set of novelty and conflict types that remains interpretable for users while still capturing most decision-relevant structure in fast-moving physics literatures?
How sensitive are belief-distortion effects to UI details such as color, ranking boosts, or wording around novelty and conflict labels?
Do the same safeguard patterns transfer to more opaque subfields (e.g., with heavy use of figures or nonstandard notation) where reliable extraction of equations and claims is harder?

question.text: If we invert the AI grad student pattern and treat human physicists as the scarce reviewers of largely AI-proposed research programs (hypotheses, derivations, simulations, literature syntheses), what minimal review protocols and stopping rules—borrowed from areas like code review, safety engineering, or clinical trial monitoring—are necessary to keep false confidence and subtle physics errors at bay, and in which regimes does this inversion demonstrably fail compared with more human-led, AI-assisted workflows?

artifacts.answer:
Summary: Treat AI as bulk producer, humans as scarce reviewers. Use simple, strict protocols and clear stopping rules. This works for incremental or well-scaffolded problems, and fails badly for novel, high-coupling, or concept-heavy work.

1) Minimal review protocols (physics-adapted)

1.1 Two-person review rule (code-review style)
- Every major AI artifact (core derivation, new hypothesis, key simulation plan, main literature synthesis) needs:
  - At least two independent human reviewers, or
  - One human + one independent AI run with different prompt/model.
- Scope: Only for claims tagged as central results; minor side calculations can be single-reviewed.
- Pass condition:
  - Both reviewers sign off on: units, limits, invariants (if applicable), and alignment with problem statement.
  - Any disagreement triggers “send back for revision” rather than compromise.

1.2 Checklists per artifact type (safety / preflight style)
- For each class:
  - Derivations: [units ok? invariants checked? key limits checked? alt route attempted?].
  - Simulations: [analytic limit test? convergence checks? seed sensitivity?].
  - Hypotheses: [clear falsifiable predictions? conflicts with prior work checked?].
  - Literature syntheses: [direct conflicts surfaced? coverage of core venues?].
- Rule: An item can only be ticked with a short text reference (equation, figure, or file line).
- No ship if any “critical” box is unticked.

1.3 Red/amber tagging with hard policies (clinical-style)
- Each major claim gets tags (reusing “epistemic safeguard” pattern from a02..., 071..., 238..., ef60..., d83...):
  - Evidence: {numerical-only, single-derivation, multi-route, analytic theorem}.
  - Review: {single-review, dual-review, unreviewed AI}.
  - Conflict: {no conflict search, conflicts unresolved, conflicts resolved}.
- Policies:
  - No “headline” results if evidence ∈ {numerical-only, single-derivation} and review ≠ dual-review.
  - No press/talk claims if conflict = no conflict search.

1.4 Fixed adversarial pass (borrowed from safety / red teaming)
- Before acceptance, one short “attack” pass:
  - Human or AI tries: extreme limits, simple toy problems, symmetry/invariance challenges, literature contradiction mining (echoing eb6...).
- Stopping rule:
  - Any unresolved counterexample or contradiction blocks acceptance.

1.5 Time-boxed review windows (clinical DMC analogy)
- For risky or surprising claims, define ahead of time:
  - Minimum review duration (e.g., 2 working days, 2 reviewers).
  - No “early approval” even if first reviewer is enthusiastic.
- Idea: avoid rushed rubber-stamping of polished AI work.

2) Stopping rules

2.1 Local stopping for artifacts
- Accept AI artifact only if:
  - All critical checklist items ticked with references.
  - At least one failed attack attempt is documented ("we tried X, got no issue").
  - Evidence tag upgraded beyond {numerical-only} where that matters, or explicitly recorded as numerical-only in write-up.

2.2 Global stopping for projects
- Trigger pause when:
  - More than N (e.g., 2–3) serious issues are found late (post-draft) that could have been caught by checklists.
  - Reviewers report they do not understand key steps but still feel pressure to approve.
- Response:
  - Narrow AI scope (e.g., no autonomous program design; use AI only for algebra/literature triage).
  - Increase human participation for the next iteration.

2.3 Escalation rules (safety / incident-style)
- If a serious bug or contradiction is found in a published or widely-shared AI-heavy result:
  - Mandatory retrospective on which protocol failed.
  - Tightening of rules for similar future artifacts (e.g., require dual-derivation for that class of calculation).

3) Concrete protocol sketches by workflow

3.1 Hypothesis/program generation
- AI may propose many research programs.
- Protocol:
  - Human pre-filters: discard proposals lacking clear falsifiable predictions.
  - For shortlisted programs, run an AI adversarial pass: auto-generate simple counter-hypotheses or alternative mechanisms.
  - Stopping rule: do not proceed if the team cannot state, in a short note, what would count as disconfirming evidence within realistic resources.

3.2 Derivations
- Protocol (building on a02..., ef60...):
  - AI does algebra; logs steps.
  - Human selects 2–3 invariance/limit checks; AI executes; humans inspect.
  - Optional: AI generates independent alternative derivation route.
- Stopping rule:
  - Any failed invariant/limit check or discrepancy between routes blocks use.
  - If no human can reproduce any nontrivial sub-step by hand (or via a CAS they trust), treat result as heuristic, not central.

3.3 Simulation planning
- Protocol (using d83...):
  - AI proposes toy models, parameter sweeps, and analytic-limit checks.
  - Humans must approve toy-model assumptions and write a 1–2 sentence “what this run is testing.”
- Stopping rule:
  - No large compute run until at least one small pilot run: validated against analytic limit or known benchmark.
  - If pilots repeatedly contradict AI’s sweep suggestions, freeze AI-driven planning and revert to simpler, human-designed scans.

3.4 Literature triage
- Protocol (using a1e39... and eb6.../071...):
  - AI clusters literature and flags likely conflicts.
  - Humans review capped “high-conflict” set.
- Stopping rule:
  - No claim may be labeled “novel” or “uncontested” until conflict search is run and high-confidence flags are manually checked.

3.5 Manuscripts and public claims
- Protocol (using 071..., 238...):
  - All major claims carry evidence and review tags in the draft.
  - One reviewer is tasked explicitly with challenging the strongest claim.
- Stopping rule:
  - If challenge reveals that claim rests mainly on unreviewed AI reasoning or unreplicated numerics, either downgrade the claim or defer submission.

4) Regimes where inverted pattern works

More suitable when:
- Problem is incremental in a well-understood framework (e.g., new parameter regime of a standard model, modest extension of known numerics).
- Strong external structure exists:
  - Clear invariants, units, scaling laws.
  - Benchmarks and analytic limits.
- AI outputs are mostly low-level (algebra, code templates, triage lists) with human-locked conceptual framing.
- Team can maintain minimal multi-person review (even if humans are “scarce,” at least two eyes see central artifacts).

5) Regimes where inversion tends to fail vs human-led workflows

5.1 Conceptually novel theories or regimes
- When key invariants, relevant degrees of freedom, or proper limits are unclear, checklists lose power.
- AI can produce plausible but fundamentally misframed programs; humans-as-reviewers see only polished surface.
- Human-led, AI-assisted workflows are better because humans generate and own the conceptual core.

5.2 High-coupling, long argument chains
- Large proofs or multi-stage pipelines where small early errors have subtle downstream effects.
- Minimal review (spot-checking) misses correlated mistakes; AI may repeat its own pattern errors in multiple artifacts.
- Human-led designs with selective AI use for local tasks and more holistic human review are safer.

5.3 Sparse or messy empirical / literature base
- In subfields with weak prior structure or noisy literature parsing (as in a1e39..., eb6... failure cases), AI triage can generate false reassurance.
- Humans may not notice missing seminal work or misread context.

5.4 High-stakes or policy-relevant claims
- Even rare subtle errors are unacceptable; minimal protocols are insufficient.
- Need deeper human involvement: independent groups, full re-derivations, external replication.

5.5 Weak organizational culture
- If incentives push speed/flash over robustness, checklists and tags become box-ticking.
- In such labs, a heavily AI-proposed workflow may amplify bad norms faster than human-led processes.

6) Main assumptions
- AI systems can reliably log steps and support simple tests (units, limits, basic invariants, simple searches).
- Human reviewers have enough domain skill to design good checks, even if they do not generate all content.
- Teams are willing to enforce basic rules (dual-review for central claims, mandatory conflict searches, no-ship on failed checks).
- Interfaces can show evidence/review tags and provenance without major friction.

7) Competing hypothesis
- The main protection against false confidence is not protocol design but constraining AI to narrow roles (algebra, basic code, keyword search). In this view, heavy AI program generation is inherently too risky; “minimal review protocols” cannot reliably compensate for the mismatch between AI fluency and true understanding, so safer labs will stick to human-led workflows with only light AI assistance.

8) Main failure case / boundary condition
- Boundary: small, fast-moving projects in unfamiliar regimes, with only one overburdened PI and no time for dual-review or adversarial passes.
- In this setting, the inverted pattern degenerates into one human lightly skimming AI-produced research programs. Checklists are ignored, conflict searches are skipped, and polished but wrong results leak into talks or preprints. Here, simpler, more conservative use of AI (as a calculator and search tool) is safer than trying to implement minimal but unenforced protocols.

claims.text: Simple, fixed review protocols—dual-review for central artifacts, short checklists per artifact type, and mandatory adversarial passes—can materially reduce false confidence in AI-heavy physics workflows when humans act mainly as reviewers.
Evidence and review tagging policies that constrain how claims can be framed or publicized are necessary to prevent AI-generated results from being silently treated as fully established findings.
The inverted pattern of AI-proposed research programs with scarce human review works best for incremental problems in well-understood frameworks with strong analytic scaffolding and benchmarks.

The inverted pattern tends to fail for conceptually novel regimes, long coupled argument chains, sparse or messy literatures, and high-stakes physics claims, where more human-led, AI-assisted workflows are safer.
Minimal review protocols cannot fully compensate for weak lab culture or strong incentives for speed, in which case AI-heavy workflows are likely to amplify rather than reduce false confidence.

assumptions: Current and near-term AI systems can reliably support units, limit, and basic invariance checking and structured logging.
Human physicists can still design and understand core checks even if they do not author all derivations or programs.
Teams are willing to adopt and enforce simple process rules that occasionally block or slow publication.
Interfaces can expose provenance and evidence tags without being routinely bypassed.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Safety in AI-supported physics comes mainly from restricting AI to narrow, well-understood tasks under strong human control; once AI is allowed to generate whole research programs, no realistic minimal review protocol can reliably prevent subtle but consequential errors, so inverted workflows are inherently inferior to human-led ones.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In small, time-pressured projects in novel regimes with only one overburdened reviewer, minimal protocols become box-ticking or are skipped; AI-generated programs are effectively unchecked, leading to polished but seriously flawed physics that would have been less likely under slower, more human-led workflows.

question.text: If we replace the AI grad student pattern with a framing where AI is treated primarily as an *evidence allocator*—whose main job is to budget human attention across hypothesis generation, derivation checking, simulation planning, and literature reading—what new failure modes emerge (e.g., systematically under-allocating scrutiny to unconventional but correct ideas, or over-focusing on regions with abundant but low-quality data), and how could we experimentally compare this allocator framing to the current protocol-enforcer / uncertainty-accountant framings on metrics like “fraction of human time spent on genuinely decision-relevant checks” and “rate of promoted but later-invalidated results”?

artifacts.answer:
New failure modes of AI-as-evidence-allocator and a compact comparison experiment:

1) New failure modes
- FM1: Popularity lock-in
  - AI routes attention toward hypotheses and parameter regions with abundant signals (papers, simulations, past discussion).
  - Risk: under-scrutiny of novel but data-sparse ideas; over-scrutiny of fashionable but low-value areas.

- FM2: Data-quality myopia
  - Allocator treats quantity or apparent consistency of evidence as quality.
  - Risk: over-focus on regions with many correlated or low-rigor results; under-focus on single but strong constraints (e.g., a clean null experiment).

- FM3: Over-trusting allocator scores
  - Teams treat attention rankings as “objective.”
  - Risk: fewer manual “off-list” checks; unconventional ideas never get minimal scrutiny.

- FM4: Metric gaming / spec creep
  - If the allocator’s objective is stated (e.g., “maximize expected impact” or “minimize invalidations”), humans and AIs adapt to it.
  - Risk: attention shifts to safe, incremental work or to regions where errors are hard to detect.

- FM5: Blind spots in uncertainty representation
  - Allocator inherits incomplete uncertainty modeling from inputs (literature models, simulations, priors).
  - Risk: whole classes of uncertainty (model misspecification, selection bias) never enter the budget, so they get no attention.

- FM6: Interface-induced anchoring
  - Ranked lists and dashboards feel more “crisp” than narrative uncertainty-accountant views.
  - Risk: users anchor on early ranks; later evidence or side-channel intuitions get discounted.

2) Experimental comparison design
Compare three framings on small, controlled tasks:
- F1: Evidence allocator (EA)
- F2: Protocol enforcer (PE)
- F3: Uncertainty accountant (UA)

2.1 Basic setup
- Use toy but nontrivial physics-style tasks:
  - Short derivations with planted subtle errors.
  - Small simulation-design problems with known edge-case failures.
  - Literature bundles with conflicting but resolvable claims.
- Randomly assign teams to EA vs PE vs UA tools.

2.2 Tool behavior (minimal sketch)
- EA: produces ranked lists of “where to spend the next hour”: which hypothesis to probe, which derivation step to check, which papers to read, which simulations to run.
- PE: surfaces checklists, invariants, assumption manifests, and required gates, but does not rank effort.
- UA: tracks uncertainty contributions (by assumption, data set, regime) and shows where marginal checks most reduce total uncertainty, but keeps ranking visually softer than EA (e.g., bands not ordered lists).

2.3 Key metrics
- M1: Fraction of human time on decision-relevant checks
  - Define “decision-relevant”: checks that change posterior over “promote / revise / drop” by a preset threshold in a ground-truth Bayesian model or expert panel judgment.
  - Measure: time spent on such checks ÷ total task time.

- M2: Rate of promoted but later-invalidated results
  - After task, reveal ground truth or run gold-standard checks.
  - Count: promoted claims that fail.
  - Normalize by number of promoted claims.

- M3: Missed high-value anomalies
  - Synthetic tasks include a small number of unconventional but correct hypotheses or regimes.
  - Measure: fraction of these that get serious attention and are correctly promoted.

- M4: Calibration and confidence spread
  - Ask participants for probabilities that their promoted claims hold.
  - Compare Brier scores and overconfidence across framings.

2.4 Detecting allocator-specific failure modes
- FM1–FM2 (lock-in, data-quality myopia)
  - Seed tasks with: (a) fashion-like clusters of many weak signals, (b) solitary but strong constraints or weird-but-correct ideas.
  - Check if EA groups:
    - Over-allocate to (a) vs (b) compared to PE/UA.
    - Have higher miss rate on seeded unconventional truths.

- FM3–FM4 (over-trust, metric gaming)
  - Give EA explicit, visible scoring criteria (e.g., “expected impact” proxy).
  - Run repeat sessions where participants can adapt.
  - Look for shift toward behavior that optimizes the score while worsening M2 or M3.

- FM5–FM6 (blind spots, anchoring)
  - Hide one important uncertainty type from all tools (e.g., model-class uncertainty) but brief some humans about it.
  - See whether EA framing makes humans less likely to override the tool based on that outside concern vs PE/UA.
  - Track how often users act on “off-rank” ideas.

2.5 Low-overhead protocol sketch
- Within-lab study:
  - 3–5 physics groups; each runs 2–3 half-day sessions.
  - Cross-over design: each group uses all three framings on different but matched tasks.
- Logging:
  - Screen recordings and click logs for time allocation.
  - Short post-task survey on perceived usefulness, trust, and felt pressure to follow rankings.

2.6 Interpretation targets
- If EA raises M1 (more time on decision-relevant checks) but also raises M2 (more invalidated promotions) and lowers M3 (misses unconventional truths), treat it as a risky default.
- If UA/PE match EA on M1 but improve M2 and M3, keep EA-style ranking only as an advisory layer with strong epistemic safeguards (e.g., forced randomization, explicit “explore off-list” quotas).

2.7 Safeguards if using evidence allocation at all
- Inject exploration:
  - Reserve a fixed fraction of attention (e.g., 10–20%) for random or human-chosen off-rank items.
- Multi-criterion views:
  - Show separate panels for “evidence richness,” “conflict density,” and “novelty” rather than a single scalar score.
- Provenance-first UI:
  - Every ranking explanation must show which signals (papers, tests, assumptions) drive it, not just a number.

This keeps the allocator framing as an experimental condition and, if adopted, embeds it inside safeguards closer to the protocol-enforcer and uncertainty-accountant styles.

claims.text: Treating AI primarily as an evidence allocator introduces distinct failure modes such as popularity lock-in, data-quality myopia, and over-trust in allocator rankings compared to protocol-enforcer or uncertainty-accountant framings.

An experimental comparison between evidence-allocator, protocol-enforcer, and uncertainty-accountant framings is feasible using small physics-style tasks with planted errors and conflicting literature bundles.

Metrics like fraction of human time spent on decision-relevant checks, rate of promoted but later-invalidated results, and detection rate of unconventional but correct hypotheses can distinguish allocator-specific risks from those of other framings.

If evidence-allocator tools increase concentration of effort on high-scoring items without explicit exploration and provenance safeguards, they are likely to systematically neglect some unconventional but correct ideas.

assumptions: Small physics-style tasks with planted errors and curated literature can approximate key aspects of real workflows.
Participants’ behavior in short experiments is informative about longer-run adoption patterns of AI tools.
Allocator, protocol-enforcer, and uncertainty-accountant behaviors can be implemented cleanly enough that differences are attributable to framing rather than UX noise.
Decision-relevance of checks can be approximated by expert judgment or simple ground-truth-based models.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: The evidence-allocator framing does not introduce qualitatively new risks; any observed failures are mainly due to poor scoring functions or UX, and with sensible design an allocator behaves similarly to a good uncertainty-accountant with prioritization features.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In real groups, users might largely ignore allocator rankings and treat all framings opportunistically, making experimental differences in metrics like invalidation rates too small or noisy to draw firm conclusions.
verification_targets: Prototype simple versions of the three framings on a shared set of toy derivation and literature tasks and measure M1–M4 across a few physics groups.
Test whether seeded unconventional-but-correct hypotheses receive less attention under the allocator framing than under protocol-enforcer or uncertainty-accountant framings.
Log and compare how often participants override tool suggestions to pursue off-rank ideas in each framing.
open_questions: How sensitive are allocator failure modes to the exact scoring objective and to adding explicit exploration quotas?
Can we design allocator explanations that reduce anchoring and over-trust enough to offset the new risks?
Do results from toy tasks transfer to large, months-long projects where attention allocation dynamics may differ?

question.text: For AI-assisted derivations in physics that already use assumption manifests and approximation flags, what additional, minimal-UI epistemic safeguards—such as requiring the AI to propose at least one concrete experiment or numerical test that would falsify the derived claim, or to generate a short “comparative failure story” describing how similar derivations broke in the literature—most effectively prevent humans from over-trusting single elegant derivation paths while keeping day-to-day workflow friction acceptable to practitioners?

artifacts.answer:
Most leverage, low-friction additions:

1) One-click falsification tests
- Safeguard: For each key result, AI must output 1–3 concrete tests (toy numerics, scaling checks, or simple experiments) with explicit parameter choices and what would count as falsification.
- UI: Collapsed “Tests” panel per result; each entry is 1–2 lines plus a code stub link.
- Effect: Shifts attention from derivation elegance to ways it could fail; easy for humans to run 1 test before trusting.

2) Comparative failure snippets
- Safeguard: AI generates a 3–5 line “nearest failure” note: one past paper or pattern where a similar approximation/limit failed and why (e.g., wrong regime, hidden nonlinearity).
- UI: Single inline icon next to each major approximation; hover shows the failure snippet and citation.
- Effect: Normalizes the idea that this step *can* fail; counters halo from a clean derivation path.

3) Alternative-branch sketch
- Safeguard: For each major approximation, AI briefly sketches one plausible alternative route (e.g., keep next order term, use different limit) and notes one qualitative prediction that would differ.
- UI: Tiny expandable “Alt path” stub (2–3 bullet points) tied to the step.
- Effect: Reminds users the shown path is one of several; discourages treating it as uniquely correct.

4) Local uncertainty badges
- Safeguard: Each high-risk step (new approximation, singular limit, heuristic closure) gets a short AI-assigned risk tag (e.g., “high regime risk”, “weak prior evidence”) with 1-line justification.
- UI: Colored dot per step; clicking filters view to all high-risk steps.
- Effect: Focuses review on where over-trust is likeliest; low overhead because tags are auto-suggested.

Practical combo:
- Default bundle for groups already using manifests/flags:
  - Per-result: 1–3 falsification tests + overall uncertainty badge.
  - Per high-risk step: failure snippet + optional alt-branch sketch.
- Keep everything collapsed by default; require humans to:
  - Run or inspect at least one falsification test, and
  - Open failure info for every high-risk step before sign-off.

This adds minimal clicks but systematically counters the “single elegant path” bias by:
- Making failure concrete and executable.
- Surfacing precedent that similar moves went wrong.
- Keeping multiple paths visible, even if only sketched.

claims.text: Requiring per-result falsification tests that are concrete, parameterized, and runnable with one click is a high-leverage, low-friction safeguard against over-trusting single elegant AI-assisted derivations.
Short comparative failure snippets tied to specific approximations or limits reduce overconfidence by reminding users that similar-looking derivation steps have failed before.
Very lightweight alternative-branch sketches at key approximations help users treat the shown derivation as one of several plausible paths rather than as uniquely correct.

Auto-generated local uncertainty badges on high-risk derivation steps help concentrate human review effort without adding substantial UI friction when layered on top of assumption manifests and approximation flags.

A combined UI pattern of falsification tests, comparative failure snippets, alternative-branch sketches, and local uncertainty badges can reduce over-trust in single derivation paths while keeping day-to-day overhead acceptable for most physics practitioners.

assumptions: Physics groups will actually run or at least inspect a small number of suggested falsification tests before trusting results.
AI systems can reliably generate simple, executable tests and retrieve at least rough precedent failures from the literature for common approximation patterns.
Minimal, collapsed UI elements do not cause significant alert fatigue when limited to a few high-risk steps per derivation.
Practitioners are willing to treat AI-assigned local uncertainty badges as prompts for extra scrutiny rather than as definitive risk scores.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: The most effective way to prevent over-trust in elegant AI-assisted derivations is not additional in-UI safeguards but stricter process norms (e.g., mandatory independent human re-derivations or code replications), and UI-level additions like tests, failure snippets, and badges add little beyond what good culture already enforces.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In very time-pressured or highly algebraic workflows, users may ignore or batch-dismiss falsification tests and failure snippets, turning safeguards into background noise while still trusting the main derivation, so overconfidence remains largely unchanged.
verification_targets: Prototype these safeguards in a small group and measure how often suggested falsification tests are actually run and how often they change user confidence or decisions.
Compare error and revision rates between derivations reviewed with and without failure snippets and uncertainty badges in otherwise similar projects.
Survey practitioners about perceived friction and usefulness after several weeks of using the combined safeguards bundle.
open_questions: How many safeguards per result is the maximum before users start to ignore them, and which single safeguard type provides the best marginal benefit?
Can AI reliably mine and summarize truly analogous past failures in subfields where errors are subtle or under-documented?
Do local uncertainty badges meaningfully improve calibration, or do users quickly learn to discount them if they are noisy?

question.text: In physics groups that already combine the AI grad student pattern with protocol-enforcer safeguards (e.g., dual-route derivations, assumption manifests, falsification tests), which *specific categories of task*—such as initial hypothesis sketching, detailed derivation expansion, simulation parameter sweeps, or “nearest-neighbor” model adaptations—still show the highest rate of AI-induced false confidence, and how can we empirically distinguish between (a) tasks that need stricter epistemic safeguards and (b) tasks that should be removed from the AI’s remit altogether?

artifacts.answer:
Main view: even with strong safeguards, the worst AI-induced false confidence clusters in (1) “nearby but actually different” problems and (2) low-salience modeling choices. Some tasks should get tighter safeguards; a smaller set should usually be off-limits. Distinguish them by measuring error detectability and human over‑reliance in small controlled trials.

1) Task categories with highest residual false-confidence risk

1.1 Nearest‑neighbor model adaptations
- Pattern: “Take model A we trust; adapt it to scenario B with small changes.”
- Typical uses: new boundary conditions, geometry tweaks, extra interaction term, new regime of same PDE.
- Risk: AI reuses the wrong assumptions/limits; derivations look familiar and polished, so humans skim.
- Why high false confidence even with safeguards:
  - Assumption manifests are often copy‑pasted from the base model.
  - Dual‑route derivations share the same hidden regime mistake.
  - Falsification tests reuse old benchmarks that don’t stress the changed regime.
- Status: keep in remit but add stronger local safeguards (see §2).

1.2 Detailed derivation expansion in conceptually new territory
- Pattern: humans outline a new mechanism/formalism; AI fills in long algebra and manipulations.
- Risk: small but structural errors (wrong sign, misapplied theorem, silent regularity assumption) that humans do not fully re-derive.
- Safeguards already present (dual routes, invariance tests) mainly catch gross inconsistencies, not subtle domain-of-validity slips.
- Status: keep in remit, but require more independent human checking and targeted tests.

1.3 Simulation parameter sweeps with complex, under‑understood models
- Pattern: AI designs/runs large sweeps; humans scan aggregate plots.
- Risk: wrong parameterization, mis-set priors, missing stability thresholds; false “phase diagrams” that look authoritative.
- Even with falsification tests, users often trust smooth plots and heatmaps.
- Status: keep in remit, but gate outputs behind stronger benchmark and sanity checks.

1.4 Hypothesis generation from biased or thin literature
- Pattern: AI proposes mechanisms extrapolating from a partial or noisy literature slice.
- With conflict maps and anomaly mining, some errors are caught, but:
  - Key nulls/contradictions can still be missing.
  - Hypotheses inherit systematic literature bias and sound more supported than they are.
- Status: keep in remit, but require explicit uncertainty labeling and minimal conflict review before promotion.

1.5 Tasks that often should be out of remit (or severely constrained)
- a) Authoritative “bottom line” judgments
  - E.g., “Is this result ready for submission?”, “Is this derivation essentially correct?”
  - These amplify deference bias; safeguards become box‑ticking.
- b) Choosing final experimental/theoretical claims language without human rewrite
  - Polished prose can overstate certainty.
- c) Silent refactoring of models or code bases without diff + tests
  - Easy to miss behavior changes.

2) Distinguishing “needs stricter safeguards” vs “should be removed”

Use small, controlled comparisons for each task type:

2.1 Metrics
- Error severity: how bad are undetected errors for downstream science?
- Error detectability: how often are errors caught by existing safeguards + normal review?
- Over‑reliance: how much does AI participation shift human confidence for wrong outputs?

2.2 Tasks needing stricter safeguards (criteria)
- Errors are common but:
  - Often caught before publication/major decisions, and
  - Human insight still clearly improves when AI is involved.
- Empirical flags that a task is in this bucket:
  - Error rate with AI > without AI, but most errors are caught in time.
  - Human confidence is moderately miscalibrated but correctable with more visibility.
- Response: keep task but add targeted safeguards, e.g.:
  - For nearest‑neighbor adaptations:
    - Mandatory “difference manifest”: short, structured list of how B differs from A and which assumptions break.
    - AI must propose at least one test specifically stressing the new regime.
  - For derivation expansion:
    - Mandatory human re‑derivation of at least one nontrivial segment.
    - Extra cross‑formalism check (e.g., Lagrangian vs Hamiltonian form) where possible.
  - For parameter sweeps:
    - Require a small, manually‑specified benchmark set that must be reproduced before scanning large grids.
    - Automatic flags when sweeps only explore narrow, AI‑chosen regions of parameter space.

2.3 Tasks to remove from remit (criteria)
- Errors are both:
  - High‑impact when they slip through, and
  - Hard for humans or secondary safeguards to detect without redoing the work.
- And: AI involvement does not reliably add unique value beyond more conservative tools.
- Empirical signatures:
  - In blinded comparisons, AI participation increases the rate of confidently wrong decisions, even after adding extra safeguards.
  - Humans tend to defer to AI even when explicitly trained not to.
- Typical outcomes under trials:
  - AI “is this ready?” judgments measurably worsen calibration.
  - AI‑written claim language systematically overstates robustness.
- Response: move these decisions to human‑only processes; allow AI to supply structured evidence (e.g., checklists, graphs, derivation trees) but not global verdicts or final phrasing.

3) Practical experiment designs to tell (a) vs (b)

3.1 Blinded A/B task runs
- For each task class (e.g., nearest‑neighbor adaptation, sweep planning):
  - Run small projects with:
    - Condition 1: AI fully involved with current safeguards.
    - Condition 2: AI involved but further constrained (extra safeguards, limited suggestions).
    - Condition 3: AI excluded; humans use ordinary tools only.
- Hide which condition teammates are in; have independent reviewers grade:
  - Presence and severity of errors.
  - Whether errors would have affected final scientific claims.
  - Human confidence vs correctness.

3.2 “Confidence delta” logging
- For each key output, log:
  - Human confidence before AI input.
  - Confidence after seeing AI’s contribution.
  - Later correctness (after stronger review or benchmark tests).
- Tasks where AI systematically increases confidence for wrong outputs, even after adjustments, are candidates for removal.

3.3 Cost–benefit traces
- For each task type, track over several projects:
  - Time saved vs human‑only.
  - Number of nontrivial ideas/paths humans would not have found.
  - Net effect on revision/bug‑fix cycles.
- If AI adds little unique value but materially worsens confidence calibration, shrink or remove remit.

Summary: the riskiest remaining zones are “looks familiar but is actually out of regime” (nearest‑neighbor adaptations, large sweeps) and “long, opaque algebra” in new territory. Most of these should stay in remit with sharper, local safeguards and explicit measurement of confidence shifts. A smaller class of high‑level judgment and language tasks is better kept human‑only, with AI limited to structured evidence rather than conclusions.

definitions.name: AI grad student pattern
definitions.meaning: A mode of AI use where the system helps with hypothesis generation, derivations, coding, and literature synthesis like a junior research collaborator.

definitions.name: epistemic safeguard
definitions.meaning: A process or interface feature that makes it easier to detect uncertainty, invalid reasoning, or unsupported scientific claims before they propagate.

claims.text: Even in workflows with dual-route derivations, assumption manifests, and falsification tests, nearest-neighbor model adaptations, detailed derivation expansions, simulation parameter sweeps, and literature-based hypothesis generation remain the main categories where AI induces false confidence in physics-style research.

Nearest-neighbor model adaptations are especially prone to AI-induced false confidence because copied assumptions, shared derivation structures, and reused benchmarks hide regime changes that existing safeguards do not always stress-test.

High-level, global judgment tasks—such as declaring results ready for publication or drafting final claims language—tend to be poor candidates for AI delegation because they systematically amplify human deference and over-trust, even under strong safeguards.

Tasks should be kept in the AI remit with stricter epistemic safeguards when AI clearly adds unique value and most errors are eventually detectable, but removed from the remit when AI both raises the rate of confidently wrong decisions and offers little unique benefit over conservative tools.

Blinded A/B task comparisons, confidence-delta logging, and simple cost–benefit traces can empirically distinguish between tasks that need stronger safeguards and tasks that should be taken out of the AI’s remit.

assumptions: Physics groups can and will run small controlled workflow experiments without prohibitive cost.
Existing safeguards like dual-route derivations, assumption manifests, and falsification tests are already reasonably well implemented.
Human reviewers can reliably grade error severity and correctness when given enough time and context.
Patterns of AI-induced false confidence are somewhat stable across projects within a given group or subfield.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Once basic safeguards are in place, residual false confidence is dominated by human culture and incentives rather than task type; attempts to micro-allocate tasks between AI and humans matter little compared with training and incentive changes.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In small or highly exploratory groups, the overhead of running controlled comparisons and adding task-specific safeguards may itself distort behavior, leading to selective adoption of the results and making the proposed remit boundaries unreliable.
verification_targets: Run a small blinded A/B study on nearest-neighbor model adaptations comparing AI-assisted vs human-only workflows, measuring both error rates and confidence calibration.
Instrument one group’s workflow to log confidence deltas and later correctness for a few task types over several months to see where AI most increases confidently wrong judgments.
Pilot removing AI from global judgment and claims-language tasks in one team and compare calibration and downstream revision rates to a similar team that keeps AI in those roles.
open_questions: How stable are task-specific false-confidence patterns across different physics subfields and group cultures?
Can interface designs meaningfully reduce deference in high-level judgment tasks, or do those tasks remain intrinsically unsafe for AI delegation?
What is the minimum experimental overhead needed to get reliable signals about which task categories should be restricted or removed from AI’s remit?

question.text: For AI-assisted simulation planning in physics, where the AI proposes parameter grids, resolution choices, and diagnostics, which minimal set of epistemic safeguards—such as automatic cross-checks against known analytic limits, stress-tests on under-resolved regimes, or prompts to justify why certain regions of parameter space are ignored—most effectively prevents “plausible but uninformative” simulation campaigns (that fail to distinguish rival hypotheses) while keeping total planning time within ~1.5× of a human-only baseline?

artifacts.answer:
A small bundle of three safeguards is likely near-minimal: (1) hypothesis-separation checks, (2) cheap regime/limit probes, and (3) ignored-region justifications, all auto-run by the AI with compact summaries.

1) Hypothesis-separation checks (core)
- For each proposed simulation set, AI must:
  - Take the rival hypotheses H1, H2, … explicitly.
  - Predict (even coarsely) a few summary observables under each Hi using cheap surrogates or analytic approximations.
  - Mark parameter regions as: “high separation”, “low separation”, or “degenerate”.
- Planning rule: a campaign is only “ready” if it contains some high-separation regions or the UI clearly states: “Plan is mostly degenerate: expected observables similar under H1/H2.”
- Effect: directly targets “plausible but uninformative” designs by forcing the question “does this plan actually distinguish anything?”
- Overhead: implemented as a background step when AI proposes grids; humans see a small separation panel, not a long report.

2) Cheap analytic-limit and under-resolution probes
- For each grid/resolution proposal the AI auto-runs:
  - Limit checks: compare to any available analytic or asymptotic results at boundaries (e.g., linear, weak-coupling, small/large parameter limits).
  - Under-resolution probes: a few toy runs or costed estimates on coarser/finer meshes (or known convergence heuristics) in selected cells tagged “risk of under-resolution”.
- Output: a compact badge per region:
  - “limit-ok/limit-unknown/limit-bad-suspect”
  - “resolution-ok/risky/unknown”
- Planning rule: regions flagged as both limit-unknown and resolution-risky either need explicit human sign-off or are visually segregated as “exploratory/low-trust”.
- Effect: reduces campaigns dominated by numerically shaky or physically irrelevant regimes without requiring full human audits.

3) Ignored-region and diagnostic-coverage justifications
- When the AI omits large chunks of parameter space or chooses only a narrow set of diagnostics, it must:
  - Emit 1–2 line reasons per major excluded region (e.g., “ruled out by prior constraint X”, “expected degeneracy for H1 vs H2”, “numerically intractable at current resources”).
  - Show a simple coverage table: observables/diagnostics × hypotheses, with cells marked “strong/weak/no discrimination expected”.
- Planning rule: before accepting, humans see at a glance:
  - Where coverage is weak.
  - Whether exclusions rely on strong assumptions versus hard constraints.
- Effect: makes quiet pruning visible and allows quick sanity checks (“we’re ignoring exactly where H2 differs most”).

Why this set is close to minimal
- All three safeguards share data and UI:
  - Hypothesis separation and coverage tables live in one small panel.
  - Limit and resolution badges appear inline on the same grid.
  - Justification stubs are 1–2 lines each.
- They mainly reuse existing ingredients in serious groups: rough models, known limits, convergence heuristics.
- Added planning time should be modest (often dominated by one extra short review pass over the AI’s panels), plausibly within ~1.5× human-only for typical projects.

Not included (to keep overhead low)
- Full Bayesian design optimization loops.
- Exhaustive robustness sweeps on many alternative grids.
- Heavy-weight anomaly mining over the literature at planning-time.

In short: require the AI to (i) show where the plan separates hypotheses, (ii) flag regimes likely to be unphysical or under-resolved, and (iii) justify big omissions. This directly targets “plausible but uninformative” campaigns with limited extra friction.

claims.text: A minimal safeguard bundle for AI-assisted simulation planning consists of explicit hypothesis-separation checks, lightweight analytic-limit and resolution probes, and short justifications for major omitted regions and diagnostics.
Requiring AI planners to label where proposed simulations strongly or weakly separate rival hypotheses directly reduces the risk of “plausible but uninformative” campaigns.

Automatic analytic-limit comparisons and simple under-resolution probes can be implemented as low-overhead checks that flag numerically or physically dubious regions without greatly increasing planning time.
Short, structured justifications for excluded parameter regions and limited diagnostic sets make hidden pruning visible and help humans catch designs that ignore the most discriminative regimes.

If these three safeguards are tightly integrated into the AI planning interface, overall simulation planning time can often be kept within roughly 1.5× of a human-only baseline in many physics workflows.

assumptions: Research teams can provide or approximate rival hypotheses and basic summary observables ahead of detailed simulations.
Rough analytic or asymptotic limits and simple convergence heuristics are available for at least parts of the parameter space.
Simulation planners are willing to review small, structured AI panels and badges as part of their normal workflow.
The AI system can access enough prior runs, models, or literature to estimate qualitative hypothesis separation without full-fidelity simulations.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Most benefit comes from better human project scoping and explicit experimental-design training; adding AI-level separation checks, limit probes, and justification prompts adds complexity with little net reduction in uninformative campaigns and may mainly create new UX friction.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In very novel or poorly understood regimes with weak analytic limits and little prior simulation experience, hypothesis separation estimates and limit/resolution badges may be highly unreliable, so safeguards either mislead users or get ignored while still adding overhead.
verification_targets: Prototype these three safeguards in one or two simulation-heavy groups and compare how often resulting plans actually produce strong hypothesis discrimination versus a baseline without the safeguards.
Measure added planning time and subjective friction when using the safeguard bundle across several projects to see if it stays within ~1.5× of human-only planning.
Audit a sample of campaigns to see whether ignored-region justifications and coverage tables actually change which parameter regions or diagnostics are selected.
open_questions: Which simple quantitative metrics for hypothesis separation (e.g., surrogate-model distance measures) are robust enough to use as planning-time signals across different physics subfields?
How noisy can limit and resolution badges be before users start ignoring them, and what calibration feedback is needed to keep them trusted but not over-trusted?
Can the same safeguard patterns be reused for experimental design as well as simulations without major redesign?

question.text: For AI-assisted derivation and simulation workflows that already use invariance tests, dual-route checks, and assumption manifests, how much additional robustness do *cross-project* safeguards provide—for example, an AI-maintained "assumption registry" that flags when a new project reuses approximations previously associated with known failures in other problems—and under what conditions (subfield maturity, similarity of governing equations, availability of negative results) does this registry meaningfully prevent the repetition of past mistakes rather than just adding noisy warnings?

artifacts.answer:
Cross-project safeguards add modest but real robustness, mainly in mature, benchmarked, equation-similar regimes with recorded failures; elsewhere they mostly create noisy alerts.

1) When an assumption registry helps most
- Conditions
  - Mature subfields with many past simulations/derivations and clear failure reports.
  - New project uses similar governing equations, asymptotics, or numerics (same PDE class, same closure or linearization tricks).
  - Negative results and near-misses are logged with assumption tags (from d81e310f) and simple outcome labels (ok / marginal / broke).
- Effects
  - Registry can give strong, low-noise warnings like: “This small-k expansion has failed in closely related turbulence runs at high Re.”
  - Typical gains: prevents reusing obviously bad approximations, narrows the safe domain of standard tricks, and highlights where extra stress tests (6e22f59d, a02bf7dd) are mandatory.
  - Added robustness: moderate; it catches repeated, pattern-like mistakes that within-project checks often miss because the local tests are too weak or mis-tuned.

2) Where it mostly adds noise
- Conditions
  - Immature or rapidly changing subfields with few well-documented failures.
  - New projects that only loosely resemble past ones (different scales, boundary conditions, or dominant physics despite similar notation).
  - Sparse or low-quality tagging of assumptions and outcomes.
- Effects
  - Many weak alerts (“this linearization once failed in a somewhat related plasma case”) that users quickly learn to ignore.
  - Risk of over-cautiousness: teams avoid standard approximations that previously failed for unrelated reasons (bad numerics, mis-specified regime) rather than the approximation itself.
  - Net robustness gain: low; culture and local safeguards dominate.

3) Conditions for meaningful error prevention vs noise
- Registry is useful when:
  - Assumptions are tagged in a small, shared vocabulary (as in d81e310f, daa702c3) and mapped to simple regime descriptors (e.g., kℓ, ωτ, Mach, Re ranges).
  - There are multiple, independent failures tied to the same assumption–regime pattern, not one-off bugs.
  - The UI enforces light gates: high-severity matches require either (a) extra tests, or (b) written override.
- It is mostly noise when:
  - Tags are fine-grained, inconsistent, or auto-inferred without review.
  - Few failures are recorded, so prior data is too thin to distinguish real red flags from coincidences.
  - Alerts are not linked to concrete next actions (extra benchmark, tighter convergence, or different approximation).

4) Practical role in the overall safeguard stack
- Best treated as a meta-level epistemic safeguard that:
  - Suggests stricter checks or alternative approximations when project–assumption–regime triples resemble past failures.
  - Feeds into planning: e.g., “This closure is flagged in high-β, low-collisionality plasmas; add dual-route derivation and extra stress-test simulations before trusting it.”
- It should not be used as a hard accept/reject oracle; rather as a trigger to strengthen existing within-project checks.

Overall: In benchmark-rich, equation-similar settings with decent negative-result logging, an assumption registry can meaningfully cut repeated mistakes (moderate robustness gain). In immature, weakly logged, or heterogeneous regimes, it mostly adds low-value caution and alert fatigue unless carefully scoped and throttled.

claims.text: Cross-project safeguards based on an AI-maintained assumption registry provide moderate additional robustness mainly in mature, benchmark-rich physics subfields with similar governing equations across projects.

An assumption registry is most effective when assumptions are tagged with a small shared vocabulary and linked to regime descriptors and multiple past failures, enabling low-noise, high-severity warnings.

In immature or heterogeneous subfields with sparse, low-quality failure logging, cross-project assumption registries mostly generate noisy, low-actionability alerts with little real impact on error rates.
Assumption registries are best used as meta-level triggers to tighten within-project safeguards, not as hard accept–reject oracles for approximations.

assumptions: Past derivations and simulations are logged with at least coarse-grained assumption and regime metadata.
Failure modes in mature subfields show some repeatable patterns tied to approximations and regimes rather than being dominated by idiosyncratic bugs.
Teams are willing to adjust local safeguard strength (extra benchmarks, tighter tests) in response to cross-project warnings.
The cost of maintaining and querying an assumption registry can be kept low relative to project size.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Most gains in avoiding repeated derivation and simulation mistakes come from improved local safeguards and human memory within each project; cross-project assumption registries add little beyond occasional anecdotal reminders and mainly create alert fatigue.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In subfields where failures are dominated by implementation bugs, mis-specified boundary conditions, or previously unknown physics rather than misapplied standard approximations, an assumption registry may mislabel safe approximations as risky and distract attention from the real error sources.

question.text: In physics groups that already use AI in the AI grad student pattern plus basic epistemic safeguards (dual-route derivations, assumption manifests, and approximation flags), which *concrete division-of-labor policies*—for example, “AI may propose but not finalize hypotheses,” “AI may draft but not restructure derivations,” or “AI may plan but not approve simulation parameter sweeps”—most reliably reduce later-invalidated results per project-hour, and how do these policies’ costs and benefits differ between (a) algebra-heavy, benchmark-rich problems and (b) concept-heavy, poorly benchmarked problems?

artifacts.answer:
High-level: In groups already using basic safeguards, the most robust extra gains come from policies that (1) keep AI in high-entropy proposal/drafting roles, (2) reserve global structuring and go/no‑go decisions for humans, and (3) make AI play an explicit adversarial/checking role at key gates. These differ by regime:

A) Algebra-heavy, benchmark-rich
- Best policies (per project-hour, for reducing later-invalidated results):
  1) Hypotheses
     - Policy H1: “AI may propose, cluster, and rank hypotheses; humans must finalize and state priors.”
     - Effect: more options and better recall; humans still own commitments. Cheap in time.
  2) Derivations
     - Policy D1: “AI may draft and locally simplify derivations; only humans may choose overall proof strategy or change problem framing.”
     - Policy D2: “AI must act as checker on any derivation it did *not* draft (dual-role separation).”
     - Effect: fewer algebraic/modeling slips; clear responsibility; good ROI because strong benchmarks and invariants exist.
  3) Simulations
     - Policy S1: “AI may propose and script parameter sweeps; humans approve parameter ranges, observables, and stopping rules.”
     - Effect: higher throughput with bounded p‑hacking; strong benchmarks make bad sweeps visible.
  4) Literature triage
     - Policy L1: “AI may triage and flag discrepancies; humans must read sources for any paper that influences claims.”

- Typical costs/benefits
  - Benefits: large drop in algebraic and configuration errors; fewer spurious ‘effects’; modest slow‑down (review gates are cheap when checks are strong).
  - Costs: human overhead in approvals and finalization; small risk of under-using AI’s ability to restructure derivations where structure is actually standard.

B) Concept-heavy, poorly benchmarked
- Best policies shift weight toward human conceptual control and AI as generator/perturbator:
  1) Hypotheses
     - Policy H2: “AI may propose and elaborate hypotheses; humans must (i) choose a small active set and (ii) define falsification tests before any heavy work.”
     - Effect: more diverse ideas; explicit pre‑commitment to tests reduces polished but unfalsifiable stories.
  2) Derivations
     - Policy D3: “AI may do local algebra and toy-model derivations; humans own global structure, approximations, and regime choices.”
     - Policy D4: “For each key approximation the AI proposes, it must also generate at least one concrete ‘how this could fail’ story; humans must sign off on proceeding anyway.”
     - Effect: slows premature lock‑in on elegant but fragile formalisms.
  3) Simulations
     - Policy S2: “AI may sketch simulation designs and parameter sweeps; humans must (i) decide which regime is decision-relevant and (ii) define success/failure criteria and pre‑registration‑style notes.”
     - Effect: fewer fishing expeditions later reinterpreted as confirmation.
  4) Literature triage
     - Policy L2: “AI may surface clusters and anomalies; humans must read at least one ‘closest contrary’ paper before promoting a mechanism.”

- Typical costs/benefits
  - Benefits: reduced over-commitment to AI-shaped framings; fewer later walk-backs based on conceptual errors; better alignment between claimed and real uncertainty.
  - Costs: more human time per idea; slower throughput; painful in early-stage, blue-sky work if over-applied.

Cross-cutting policies (useful in both regimes)
- C1: Role separation
  - “The AI instance that proposes hypotheses/derivations/sweeps cannot be the one that performs primary checking; a second AI (or human) must attack the output.”
- C2: Human-only decision gates
  - “Only humans may (i) declare a result ‘ready for external communication’ or (ii) retire a competing hypothesis as ‘ruled out.’”
- C3: AI-as-adversary at promotion time
  - “Before any result crosses a publication or talk threshold, an AI must be tasked explicitly to generate counterarguments, alternative explanations, and failure modes; a human must respond in writing.”

Regime contrast summary
- Algebra-heavy, benchmark-rich:
  - Let AI be aggressive in drafting, algebra, coding, and sweep design; constrain it mainly at approval and restructuring stages. Use dual-role AI checkers heavily. Gains are large and cheap because benchmarks quickly surface errors.
- Concept-heavy, poorly benchmarked:
  - Keep AI in idea-generation, local-calculation, and framing-perturbation roles. Reserve problem framing, approximation choices, and hypothesis promotion to humans, with explicit falsification tests. Gains come mostly from avoiding overconfident narratives, at the cost of slower exploration.

Net: Policies that (i) give AI high-bandwidth proposal and drafting authority, (ii) reserve structural and commitment decisions to humans, and (iii) enforce creator–checker separation (including AI adversaries) are the most promising for lowering later-invalidated results per project-hour, with more permissive drafting in benchmark-rich algebraic work and stricter human control in concept-heavy, poorly benchmarked work.

claims.text: In algebra-heavy, benchmark-rich physics problems, division-of-labor policies that allow AI to draft derivations and plan simulation sweeps while reserving restructuring and approval decisions for humans reduce later-invalidated results per project-hour with modest time cost.

Creator–checker separation between AI roles—where one AI drafts and another independently attacks derivations or simulation plans—substantially reduces shared human–AI errors compared with using a single AI instance for both tasks.

In concept-heavy, poorly benchmarked physics problems, policies that keep AI in proposal and local-algebra roles while requiring humans to control framing, approximation choices, and hypothesis promotion reduce overconfident but fragile results at the expense of slower throughput.

Requiring human-only gates for finalizing hypotheses and approving simulation parameter sweeps, combined with AI-generated counterarguments at promotion time, is a generally robust safeguard against AI-induced false confidence across both benchmark-rich and poorly benchmarked regimes.

assumptions: Physics groups can and will follow explicit written division-of-labor policies over months-long projects.
Existing benchmarks and invariants in algebra-heavy domains are strong enough that most important errors eventually surface if AI is constrained at approval points.
Human researchers are willing to invest some extra time in approvals, falsification-test design, and reading primary sources.
AI systems are at least moderately competent at algebra, code generation, and literature retrieval but remain prone to subtle conceptual and modeling errors.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Once basic safeguards like dual-route derivations and assumption manifests are in place, fine-grained division-of-labor policies about who may restructure derivations or approve sweeps add little marginal benefit; overall error rates are driven mainly by human culture, incentives, and the strength of external benchmarks.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In small, time-pressured groups, strict policies that reserve many gates for humans may be applied only on high-visibility projects; for typical work they are bypassed, so added complexity yields little real reduction in invalidated results while encouraging informal workarounds.
verification_targets: Run controlled, small-group trials comparing error and retraction rates under more vs less restrictive AI restructuring/approval policies in a benchmark-rich numerical physics task suite.
Audit concept-heavy projects using AI with and without human-only hypothesis finalization and explicit AI adversary gates, measuring how often promoted results later require conceptual revision.
Log how often creator–checker AI separation actually leads to newly-discovered issues that would have been missed by a single-instance AI in real workflows.
open_questions: What is the minimal set of division-of-labor rules that yields most of the safety benefits without noticeable slow-down in typical groups?
How do these policies interact with junior human training—do they improve or degrade students’ ability to do independent conceptual work?
Can we design simple quantitative metrics (e.g., ‘invalidations per promoted result’) that groups can track to tune how strict their AI division-of-labor policies should be?

question.text: If we treat the AI grad student pattern as assuming that *local project context* is the right unit for AI collaboration, how does the picture change if we instead treat the AI as a cross-group, cross-subfield “error pattern miner” whose primary job is to surface systematic derivation, modeling, and simulation planning pathologies seen in other teams’ work (e.g., common misuses of limits, recurring benchmark failures) back into a given project—and in controlled comparisons, does this error-miner framing more effectively reduce false confidence than adding further within-project safeguards like richer checklists or stricter creator/checker separation?

artifacts.answer:
Treating the AI as a cross-project error-pattern miner mainly adds a new *axis* of defense (reuse of past failures) rather than outright dominating strong within-project safeguards. It is most useful where governing equations and numerical tricks repeat across groups, and where past failures are logged well enough to mine.

1) How the picture changes under an error-pattern–miner framing
- Scope: the AI works over many projects, hunting recurring failure modes in derivations, modeling choices, and simulation plans.
- Main roles:
  - Match current approximations, limits, and discretizations to a library of past failures (misused limits, unstable schemes, misleading benchmarks).
  - Surface “this went wrong before in nearby problems” alerts early in derivations and simulation planning.
- Compared to AI grad-student pattern:
  - Less local creativity, more reuse of global negative experience.
  - Moves some epistemic safeguards from per-project checklists to cross-project pattern recognition.

2) When cross-project error mining helps more than extra local safeguards
- Strongest gains when:
  - Subfield is mature and equation-similar across groups (e.g., same PDE class, standard closures).
  - There is a history of documented failures (misapplied small-parameter limits, under-resolved turbulence, bad boundary conditions).
  - Within-project safeguards already exist (invariance tests, dual-route checks, assumption manifests) but still miss *systematic* traps because local tests are mis-tuned.
- In this regime, error mining:
  - Flags approximations and schemes with a track record of breaking, prompting stricter local checks or alternate methods.
  - Reduces false confidence mainly by preventing re-use of historically fragile tricks that local checklists treat as routine.
- Added value over just richer checklists or stricter creator/checker:
  - Moderate: it targets *external* failure information that local structure alone cannot see.

3) When extra within-project safeguards help more
- In immature, heterogeneous, or theory-novel regimes:
  - Few comparable past projects and little clean failure logging.
  - Many errors are conceptual or domain-specific, not repetitions of standard pathologies.
- Here, stronger local safeguards (typed AI outputs, assumption manifests, invariance tests, dual-route derivations, creator/checker separation) usually:
  - Catch more issues than a high-noise error miner.
  - Reduce false confidence by forcing explicit articulation and local challenge rather than relying on thin cross-project history.

4) Controlled comparisons (idealized)
- Benchmark-rich, repetition-heavy regimes:
  - Baseline: AI grad-student + standard local safeguards.
  - Treatment A: add richer checklists + stricter creator/checker.
  - Treatment B: add cross-project error miner that injects warnings when known-bad patterns appear.
  - Expected pattern:
    - A and B both reduce error rates; B especially cuts *repeat* failures (same bad limit or discretization) that A still sometimes permits.
    - Overall false-confidence reduction: error miner modestly better or comparable, but only where its failure library is dense.
- Benchmark-poor or novel regimes:
  - Error miner has few strong patterns; warnings are generic or noisy.
  - Creator/checker separation, typing, and explicit assumption handling likely out-perform cross-project mining on both error rate and calibration.

5) Practical combination
- Best use is layered:
  - Keep lean but strong within-project safeguards (as in a02bf7dd, d81e310f, 6e22f59d).
  - Add error-miner AI as a *meta-trigger*:
    - “This approximation/scheme has failed N times in similar regimes; upgrade checks here (extra benchmarks, finer grids, dual-route).”
  - Treat error-miner alerts as prompts to strengthen local gates, not as an oracle.
- Net effect: cross-project error mining complements but does not replace well-designed within-project structures; its main distinct benefit is suppressing repeated, pattern-like failures that local teams misjudge as safe.

claims.text: AI used as a cross-project error-pattern miner adds the most epistemic value in mature, equation-similar physics subfields with many logged failures, by flagging historically fragile approximations and schemes that local teams treat as routine.

In immature or highly heterogeneous physics regimes, strengthening within-project safeguards like assumption manifests, invariance tests, and creator–checker separation usually reduces false confidence more than adding a cross-project error miner.

Cross-project error mining and within-project safeguards are complementary: the error miner is best used as a trigger to tighten local checks around historically fragile patterns, not as a standalone accept–reject mechanism.

In controlled comparisons within benchmark-rich, repetition-heavy workflows, adding a cross-project error miner is likely to reduce repeat-pattern failures more than simply enriching local checklists, but with only modest overall gains in error rates and confidence calibration.

assumptions: Enough projects in a subfield log derivation and simulation failures in a structured way to support pattern mining.
Error patterns in approximations and numerical schemes generalize meaningfully across projects that share governing equations.
Teams are willing to act on AI-surfaced error-pattern warnings by tightening local safeguards rather than ignoring them.
Within-project safeguards can be implemented with reasonably low overhead so they are actually used.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Most practical reduction in false confidence comes from improving local safeguards and human review culture; cross-project error-pattern mining adds little beyond occasional anecdotal reminders and may mainly create noisy alerts that teams learn to ignore.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In subfields where serious errors are dominated by novel conceptual mistakes, implementation bugs, or new physical effects rather than reuses of known-bad approximations or schemes, an error-pattern miner will rarely trigger on the most important failures and may provide a false sense that known pitfalls are the main risk.

question.text: Current work mostly assumes the AI grad student pattern plus added epistemic safeguards inside a project; what changes if we instead treat *research group epistemology itself* as the main target, and design AI primarily as a “norm enforcer” that monitors sequences of hypotheses, derivations, simulations, and claims over months to flag patterns like systematic under-reporting of negative results, repeated reuse of fragile approximations, or selective citation of supportive literature—and in controlled comparisons, does this lab-level norm-enforcer framing reduce false confidence and low-quality physics outputs more than further improving per-derivation safeguards within the existing AI grad student workflow?

artifacts.answer:
Lab-level AI norm-enforcer framing mainly shifts focus from single derivations to long-run patterns in how a group reasons and reports. It likely helps most in bias-prone, high-throughput labs, but complements rather than replaces per-derivation safeguards.

Key changes vs AI grad student + local safeguards
- Scope: track projects over months (hypotheses, sims, papers) instead of just local derivations.
- Targets: lab-wide patterns—missing nulls, recurring fragile approximations, selective citation—rather than single-step algebraic errors.
- Role: AI acts as “process auditor” and dashboard builder, not a creative teammate.

1) What the norm-enforcer does
- Longitudinal logging
  - Auto-tag hypotheses, derivations, sims, and claims with simple metadata: status, approximations used, result sign (supporting / null / contrary), key references.
  - Maintain a lab history: how many ideas died, which approximations recur, which references dominate.
- Pattern flags (examples)
  - Null-result under-reporting: flagged if many internal nulls but few in writeups.
  - Fragile-approx reuse: same approximation repeatedly used outside its validated regime.
  - Citation skew: strong over-use of supportive papers vs known constraints or nulls.
  - Confirmation-heavy sims: most campaigns favor one hypothesis; few are designed to discriminate.
- Safeguard surface
  - Simple periodic reports: “Last 6 months: 15 projects, 2 nulls written up; 80% of main results use X-approx outside tested range.”
  - Soft gates: before submission, checklist derived from these stats (“You have 4 unreported nulls touching this claim”).

2) When lab-level norm enforcement helps more than just better local safeguards
- Likely high value when
  - Group runs many related projects; same models/approximations recur.
  - Publication pressure is strong; negative results easily dropped.
  - There is at least basic instrumentation (issue tracker, lab wiki, internal preprints) for the AI to hook into.
- Gains vs local safeguards
  - Catches slow-drift cultural problems (e.g., optimism bias, quiet pruning of nulls) that are invisible in any single derivation.
  - Makes systematic overuse of a dubious approximation visible across projects, even if each derivation “passes” local checks.
